title: ISO 27001 Segregation of Duties вЂ” Privileged Splunk Knowledge Object Changes

compliance.assurance_rationale: ISO/IEC 27001 A.5.3 вЂ” Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that ISO 27001 A.5.3 is enforced вЂ” Splunk UC-22.6.8: ISO 27001 Segregation of Duties вЂ” Privileged Splunk Knowledge Object Changes.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.6.8' running on index=_audit (object_type, action, user, object, info), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted вЂ” SME review required.

spl:
index=_audit object_type IN ("savedsearch","alert_actions","transforms","props","authorize") action IN ("create","update","delete") earliest=-30d
| eval object_name=coalesce(object, info)
| stats count as changes, values(action) as actions, dc(object_type) as object_types by user
| lookup splunk_privileged_users.csv user OUTPUT is_breakglass
| where is_breakglass="true" OR changes>25
| sort - changes
description: Conflicting duties must be separated to reduce fraud and error. Auditing edits to high-risk Splunk objects by the same accounts that can execute destructive searches evidences compensating detective controls where native SoD is limited.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for ISO 27001 compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes _audit. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
grandmaExplanation: We keep evidence for Segregation of Duties вЂ” Privileged Splunk Knowledge Object Changes. ISO 27001 tells us what good security management looks like, and we document that we follow it.
implementation: (1) Maintain `splunk_privileged_users.csv` for admin and break-glass IDs; (2) forward `_audit` from all search heads in the cluster; (3) alert on deletes to `authorize` or `props`; (4) pair with change tickets via `user`в†’`snow_sys_id` lookup; (5) include in role recertification for A.5.3 (segregation of duties). Indexes required: `index=_audit`.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
вЂў Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Enterprise core auditing (`_audit` index).
вЂў Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=_audit` (object_type, action, user, object, info).
вЂў For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 вЂ” Configure data collection
(1) Maintain `splunk_privileged_users.csv` for admin and break-glass IDs; (2) forward `_audit` from all search heads in the cluster; (3) alert on deletes to `authorize` or `props`; (4) pair with change tickets via `user`в†’`snow_sys_id` lookup; (5) include in role recertification for A.5.3 (segregation of duties).

Step 2 вЂ” Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=_audit object_type IN ("savedsearch","alert_actions","transforms","props","authorize") action IN ("create","update","delete") earliest=-30d
| eval object_name=coalesce(object, info)
| stats count as changes, values(action) as actions, dc(object_type) as object_types by user
| lookup splunk_privileged_users.csv user OUTPUT is_breakglass
| where is_breakglass="true" OR changes>25
| sort - changes
```

Understanding this SPL

**ISO 27001 Segregation of Duties вЂ” Privileged Splunk Knowledge Object Changes (A.5.3)** вЂ” Conflicting duties must be separated to reduce fraud and error. Auditing edits to high-risk Splunk objects by the same accounts that can execute destructive searches evidences compensating detective controls where native SoD is limited.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=_audit` (object_type, action, user, object, info). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise core auditing (`_audit` index). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feedвЂ”rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: _audit.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

вЂў Scopes the data: index=_audit, time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
вЂў `eval` defines or adjusts **object_name** вЂ” often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
вЂў `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by user** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
вЂў Enriches events using `lookup` (lookup definition + optional OUTPUT fields).
вЂў Filters the current rows with `where is_breakglass="true" OR changes>25` вЂ” typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
вЂў Orders rows with `sort` вЂ” combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.


Step 3 вЂ” Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with the GRC system, audit register, or framework crosswalk. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 вЂ” Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (user, changes, actions), Timeline (edits by object_type), Single value (delete actions count).
visualization: Table (user, changes, actions), Timeline (edits by object_type), Single value (delete actions count).
